docs.title: Architecture Vulnerability Analysis for 64-bit Microcontroller Design
About This Documentation
docs.text:
This documentation presents a technical analysis of transient execution vulnerabilities, specifically Meltdown and Spectre, in the context of a 64-bit single-core RISC-V microcontroller design.

The goal of this work is to:

  • Explain how modern CPU microarchitectural features introduce security vulnerabilities
  • Analyze the architectural requirements for Meltdown and Spectre attacks
  • Evaluate whether these vulnerabilities apply to a minimal RISC-V microcontroller
  • Demonstrate why and where these attacks fail in a simplified architecture
  • Explore residual risks (e.g., cache timing side-channels) and mitigation strategies

This documentation is based on a structured research study and has been adapted into an interactive format using MkDocs for better readability and navigation.

docs.title: Scope of the Document
docs.text:
This documentation focuses on:

  • Transient execution attacks (Meltdown & Spectre)
  • Microarchitectural concepts:
    • Out-of-order execution
    • Speculative execution
    • Branch prediction
    • Cache-based side channels
  • RISC-V architecture (RV64I) in a microcontroller context
  • Security implications of:
    • Absence of virtual memory
    • Lack of privilege separation
    • Shared cache behavior

docs.title: How to Use This Documentation
docs.text: Depending on your goal, you can navigate this documentation in different ways:

docs.title: For First-Time Readers
docs.text:
Start with:

  1. Abstract → Overview of the work
  2. Background → Understand Meltdown & Spectre fundamentals
  3. Architecture → Learn about the RISC-V design

docs.title: For Technical Deep Dive
docs.text:
Jump to:

  • Attack Mechanisms → Step-by-step execution of attacks
  • RISC-V Failure Analysis → Why attacks fail on this design
  • Countermeasures → Hardware & software mitigation strategies

docs.title: For Quick Understanding
docs.text:
Read:

  • Vulnerability Analysis Summary
  • Conclusions

docs.title: Document Structure
docs.text: The documentation is organized into the following sections:

docs.title: 1. Overview
docs.text: Overview of the research objectives, methodology, and key findings.

docs.title: 2. Background
docs.text:
• Meltdown (CVE-2017-5754)
  • Spectre (CVE-2017-5753, CVE-2017-5715)
  • Cache timing side-channel (Flush+Reload)

docs.title: 3. Architectural Preconditions
docs.text: Defines the necessary hardware conditions required for these attacks.

docs.title: 4. RISC-V Microcontroller Design
docs.text:
Describes the target system architecture, including:

  • Pipeline structure
  • Cache configuration
  • Privilege model

docs.title: 5. Vulnerability Analysis
docs.text:
Explains:

  • Why Meltdown is not applicable
  • Why Spectre is limited or conditional

docs.title: 6. Attack Mechanisms (Reference Systems)
docs.text:
Step-by-step explanation of how:

  • Meltdown works on privileged processors
  • Spectre exploits branch prediction

docs.title: 7. RISC-V Attack Failure Analysis
docs.text:
A detailed breakdown of why attacks fail on the microcontroller, including:

  • Absence of privilege boundary
  • No page fault mechanism
  • Flat memory model

docs.title: 8. Countermeasures
docs.text:
• Software-level mitigations
  • Hardware-level protections
  • Industry-standard defenses

docs.title: 9. Conclusions
docs.text:
Key takeaways regarding:

  • Architectural security vs complexity
  • Trade-offs in microcontroller design

docs.title: 10. References
docs.text: Academic papers, specifications, and industry resources used in this work.

docs.title: Quick Navigation
docs.text: Section Description Abstract Overview of the research Meltdown Transient execution attack via privilege bypass Spectre Speculative execution via branch misprediction Preconditions Required architectural features RISC-V Design Target microcontroller architecture Vulnerability Analysis Applicability of attacks Meltdown Attack Attack flow on real CPUs Spectre Attack Branch predictor exploitation Failure Analysis Why attacks fail Countermeasures Defense strategies Conclusion Final insights References Sources

docs.title: Key Takeaway
docs.text:
Security vulnerabilities like Meltdown and Spectre are not just implementation flaws — they are consequences of architectural design choices.

This documentation highlights how a minimalist RISC-V microcontroller architecture can inherently eliminate entire classes of vulnerabilities by removing the conditions required for their existence.

docs.title: Intended Audience
docs.text:
This documentation is intended for:

  • Embedded systems engineers
  • Computer architecture students
  • Hardware security researchers
  • RISC-V developers
  • Anyone interested in microarchitectural security

docs.title: Notes
docs.text:
• This documentation assumes basic familiarity with:

  • Computer architecture concepts

  • C programming
  • CPU pipeline fundamentals
  • All examples are simplified for clarity but retain technical accuracy.

docs.title: Conclusion
docs.text: Skip to TL;DR

docs.title: Summary of Findings
docs.text:
This work evaluated the applicability of Meltdown and Spectre on a 64-bit RISC-V microcontroller by analyzing required architectural preconditions and mapping them to the implemented design .

The results show that vulnerability is not determined solely by:

  • Speculative execution
  • Pipeline depth
  • Cache hierarchy

but by the interaction between execution, isolation, and observability.

docs.title: Meltdown
docs.text:
• Requires:

    • Hardware-enforced privilege boundary
    • Page fault mechanism
    • Deferred exception handling

  • In the analyzed design:

    • No privilege separation
    • No virtual memory
    • No page faults
Note

The fundamental condition required to create a transient fault window is absent.

Result:

  • Meltdown is structurally impossible

docs.title: Variant 2 (BTB Injection)
docs.text:
• Requires:
    • Separate execution contexts
    • Shared predictor state

Result:

  • Not applicable due to absence of context isolation

docs.title: Variant 1 (Bounds Check Bypass)
docs.text:
• Requires:
    • Predictable branch
    • Shared cache
    • Isolation boundary

Result:

  • Not applicable in single trust domain
  • Conditionally possible in multi-component software systems
Warning

The hardware does not prevent cross-component leakage when software isolation is absent.

docs.title: Cache Timing Side-Channel
docs.text:
• Cache is:
    • Present
    • Shared
    • Timing observable

Result:

  • Side-channel remains fully functional

docs.title: Architectural Interpretation
docs.text:
The microcontroller demonstrates:

  • Immunity to Meltdown
  • Elimination of Spectre V2 attack surface
  • Reduced Spectre V1 scope
  • Retention of timing side-channels

This is achieved not through mitigation, but through absence of required attack preconditions.

docs.title: Design Insight
docs.text:
flowchart TD
A[Speculative Execution] --> B[Transient Effects]
B --> C[Leakage Channel]
C --> D[Data Exposure]

E[Remove Isolation] --> F[No Boundary to Cross]
F --> G[Meltdown Eliminated]

H[Remove Context Separation] --> I[No Victim Target]
I --> J[Spectre V2 Eliminated]

docs.title: Security Trade-off
docs.text:
The design reflects a fundamental trade-off:

Aspect Effect No privilege separation Eliminates Meltdown No virtual memory Removes protected regions Shared cache Enables side-channel OoO execution Enables speculation Note

Security is achieved by reducing architectural complexity rather than adding defensive mechanisms.

docs.title: Practical Implications
docs.text:
• For bare-metal microcontrollers:

    • Meltdown-class attacks are irrelevant
    • Spectre risk depends on software architecture

  • For multi-component systems:

    • Software must enforce isolation
    • Side-channel-aware design is required

docs.title: Final Insight
docs.text: Transient execution vulnerabilities are not universal flaws in speculative execution—they are consequences of combining speculation with isolation boundaries and observable microarchitectural state.

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Meltdown → Not applicable

    • No privilege boundary
    • No page fault mechanism

  • Spectre V2 → Not applicable

    • No separate execution context

  • Spectre V1 → Conditionally possible

    • Only with shared software components

  • Cache timing side-channel → Present

  • Core takeaway:

    • Vulnerability = Speculation + Isolation + Observability

The analyzed RISC-V microcontroller achieves security through architectural minimalism, eliminating attack preconditions rather than mitigating them.

docs.title: Countermeasures
docs.text: Skip to TL;DR

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Although Meltdown is not applicable to the analyzed RISC-V microcontroller, Spectre-class behavior and cache timing side-channels remain relevant.

This section presents:

  • Countermeasures applicable to the target microcontroller design
  • Reference countermeasures used in privileged processors for comparison

docs.title: Design Perspective
docs.text:
The microcontroller achieves immunity to Meltdown and Spectre V2 by design, not by mitigation:

  • No privilege boundary → no Meltdown
  • No context separation → no Spectre V2

However:

  • Speculative execution → present
  • Branch prediction → present
  • Cache side-channel → present

Therefore:

  • Spectre V1 and timing leakage must be mitigated
Note

Countermeasures focus on reducing speculation-based leakage and side-channel observability.

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
• Use RISC-V:

    • fence → serializes memory operations
    • fence.i → serializes instruction fetch

docs.title: Effect
docs.text:
• Prevents speculative execution beyond the barrier
  • Forces pipeline to resolve prior instructions
if (x < size) {
    asm volatile ("fence");
    val = array[x];
}
Warning

Overuse introduces pipeline stalls and performance degradation.

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
Replace:

if (x < size)
    val = array[x];

With:

x = x & (size - 1);
val = array[x];

docs.title: Effect
docs.text:
• Eliminates conditional branch
  • Removes branch predictor dependency
  • Prevents misprediction-based leakage
Tip

Effective for power-of-two array sizes.

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
• Access all possible elements regardless of condition
  • Use arithmetic masking instead of branching

docs.title: Effect
docs.text:
• Removes data-dependent timing variation
  • Prevents cache-based inference
Note

Common in cryptographic implementations.

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
• Disable user access to cycle counter:
// mcounteren.CY = 0 (machine configuration)

docs.title: Effect
docs.text:
• Reduces timing resolution
  • Makes cache timing attacks less reliable
Warning

Does not eliminate side-channel—only increases noise.

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
• Partition cache ways between software components

Example:

  • 2-way cache → assign 1 way per task

docs.title: Effect
docs.text:
• Prevents cross-component cache observation
  • Eliminates shared cache channel
Warning

Requires hardware modification to cache controller.

docs.title: Suppress Cache Fill on Speculative Instructions
Mechanism
docs.text:
• Prevent cache updates from instructions that:

    • Are executed speculatively
    • Are later flushed (ROB discard)
flowchart LR
A[Speculative Load] --> B{Commit?}
B -->|Yes| C[Allow Cache Fill]
B -->|No| D[Suppress Cache Update]

docs.title: Effect
docs.text:
• Eliminates leakage from transient execution
  • Breaks Meltdown/Spectre exfiltration channel
Tip

This is a hardware-level mitigation aligning microarchitectural state with architectural commit.

docs.title: Applicability Summary (This Design)
docs.text: Countermeasure Target Effectiveness Fence instructions Spectre V1 High Index masking Spectre V1 High Constant-time access Side-channel High Timer restriction Side-channel Medium Cache partitioning Side-channel High Speculative cache suppression All Very high

docs.title: Reference Countermeasures (Privileged CPUs)
docs.text: These are not required for the current design but provide context.

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
• Separate user and kernel page tables
  • Remove kernel mappings from user space

docs.title: Effect
docs.text:
• Prevents Meltdown by eliminating mapped kernel pages
Warning

Introduces TLB flush overhead on context switches.

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text: • Replace indirect branches with return-based trampolines

docs.title: Effect
docs.text: • Prevents BTB poisoning (Spectre V2)

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text: • Restrict sharing of branch predictor state across contexts

docs.title: Effect
docs.text: • Mitigates cross-privilege speculation

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text: • Insert serialization barrier after bounds checks

docs.title: Effect
docs.text: • Prevents speculative execution past critical checks

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
• Predictor isolation
  • Speculation restrictions in hardware

docs.title: Effect
docs.text: • Eliminates need for software workarounds

docs.title: Design Insight
docs.text:
flowchart TD
A[Speculative Execution] --> B[Cache State Change]
B --> C[Timing Observation]
C --> D[Data Leakage]

E[Countermeasures] --> F[Break Chain]
F -->|Barrier| A
F -->|Isolation| B
F -->|Noise| C
  • Countermeasures operate by breaking any link in the leakage chain

docs.title: Key Observations
docs.text:
• Eliminating privilege boundary removes Meltdown
  • Eliminating branch misprediction influence reduces Spectre
  • Eliminating cache observability removes leakage
Note

Complete security requires addressing all three dimensions: execution, isolation, and observability.

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Meltdown → no mitigation required (not applicable)

  • Spectre V1 → mitigate via:

    • Fence instructions
    • Index masking
    • Constant-time access

  • Cache side-channel → mitigate via:

    • Timer restriction
    • Cache partitioning

  • Strongest defense:

    • Suppress cache updates from speculative execution

The most effective countermeasure is architectural: aligning microarchitectural state changes with instruction commit semantics.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: Skip to TL;DR

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Transient execution attacks such as Meltdown and Spectre exploit the gap between:

  • Architectural correctness (what the CPU commits)
  • Microarchitectural behavior (what happens during execution)

Modern processors expose this gap due to:

  • Out-of-order execution
  • Speculative execution
  • Branch prediction
  • Shared cache hierarchies

These features introduce observable side-effects (e.g., cache state changes) that persist even when instructions are not architecturally committed.

docs.title: Target Architecture
docs.text:
The analysis is performed on a 64-bit RISC-V microcontroller with the following properties:

Component Description ISA RV64I Execution Model In-order issue, OoO execution (ROB-based) Privilege Levels Single mode (no U/S separation) Memory Model Physical addressing only Cache 2-way set-associative Branch Predictor Present Exception Handling Machine-mode only

docs.title: Threat Model
docs.text:
The attacker:

  • Executes arbitrary code on the system
  • Controls inputs to target code paths
  • Measures execution timing (e.g., cycle counter)

The objective is to extract data via:

  • Privilege boundary violation (Meltdown)
  • Speculative execution misuse (Spectre)

docs.title: Attack Preconditions
docs.text:
The feasibility of transient execution attacks depends on the following:

Precondition Role Speculative / OoO execution Enables transient execution window Branch predictor Required for Spectre Shared cache Enables timing side-channel Privilege boundary Required for Meltdown Isolated execution context Required for Spectre V2 Note

The absence of any required precondition invalidates the corresponding attack class.

docs.title: Meltdown: Applicability
docs.text:
Meltdown requires:

  • A hardware-enforced privilege boundary
  • A faulting memory access delayed by speculative execution

In the target system:

  • No virtual memory
  • No page tables
  • No user/kernel separation

As a result:

  • No access generates a privilege fault
  • No protected memory region exists
Warning

Any memory location can be accessed directly. This eliminates the need for a Meltdown-style bypass.

docs.title: Spectre: Applicability
docs.text:
Spectre relies on:

  • Mispredicted branches
  • Speculative execution of unintended paths
  • Cache-based side-channel leakage

In the target system:

  • Branch predictor → Present
  • OoO execution → Present
  • Cache → Present

However:

  • No hardware isolation between software components
Note

Spectre Variant 1 remains possible in scenarios with shared software execution (e.g., cooperative multitasking).

Note

Spectre Variant 2 is not applicable due to absence of a separate privileged context.

docs.title: Transient Execution Leakage Model
docs.text:
flowchart LR
A["Speculative Execution"] --> B["Transient Data Access"]
B --> C["Cache State Update"]
C --> D["Rollback (Architectural State)"]
D --> E["Timing Measurement"]
E --> F["Data Recovery"]
Tip

The cache state is not rolled back after speculative execution, making it a persistent side-channel.

docs.title: Architectural Implications
docs.text:
The analyzed microcontroller exhibits:

  • Flat memory model → No access control
  • Uniform privilege level → No isolation boundary
  • Observable cache behavior → Timing side-channel present

This leads to:

  • Elimination of Meltdown attack surface
  • Reduction of Spectre attack scope
  • Retention of cache-based leakage mechanisms

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Meltdown is not applicable
    • No privilege boundary
    • No page fault mechanism
  • Spectre V2 is not applicable
    • No separate execution context
  • Spectre V1 is conditionally possible
    • Requires shared software execution
  • Cache timing side-channel exists
    • Due to shared cache

Security properties are determined by architectural composition. Removing privilege separation eliminates entire vulnerability classes rather than mitigating them.

docs.title: Core Research Papers
docs.text:
[1]{#ref-1} Lipp, M., Schwarz, M., Gruss, D., Prescher, T., Haas, W., Horn, J., Mangard, S. Meltdown: Reading Kernel Memory from User Space. USENIX Security Symposium, 2018.

[2]{#ref-2} Kocher, P., Horn, J., Fogh, A., Genkin, D., Gruss, D., Haas, W., Yarom, Y. Spectre Attacks: Exploiting Speculative Execution. IEEE Symposium on Security and Privacy, 2019.

docs.title: Architecture Specifications
docs.text:
[3]{#ref-3} RISC-V International The RISC-V Instruction Set Manual, Volume II: Privileged Architecture. Version 20211203, 2021.

[4]{#ref-4} RISC-V International RISC-V Cache Management Operation ISA Extensions (Zicbom). Version 1.0, 2022.

docs.title: Industry Whitepapers
docs.text:
[5]{#ref-5} Intel Corporation Analysis of Speculative Execution Side Channels. White Paper, Revision 4.0, 2018.

[6]{#ref-6} ARM Limited Cache Speculation Side-channels. Whitepaper, Version 2.4, 2018.

docs.title: Side-Channel Research
docs.text: [7]{#ref-7} Gruss, D., Maurice, C., Fogh, A., Mangard, S. Flush+Flush: A Fast and Stealthy Cache Attack. DIMVA, 2016.

docs.title: Textbooks
docs.text: [8] Patterson, D. A., Hennessy, J. L. Computer Organization and Design: RISC-V Edition. Morgan Kaufmann, 2017.

docs.title: Notes
docs.text: References are grouped by type: foundational papers, architecture specifications, industry analysis, and supporting literature.

docs.title: Architectural Preconditions
docs.text: Skip to TL;DR

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
Meltdown and Spectre are not universally applicable vulnerabilities. Their feasibility depends on the presence of specific microarchitectural and architectural features.

Three independent conditions must hold simultaneously for these attacks to be exploitable .

docs.title: Required Preconditions
docs.text:
Precondition Role in Attack Effect if Absent Speculative / Out-of-Order Execution Enables execution before instruction validity is resolved No transient execution window Hardware Branch Predictor Enables speculative control-flow redirection No controllable misprediction Shared, Observable Cache Provides timing-based leakage channel No observable side-channel Privilege Separation (Meltdown-specific) Defines protected vs unprotected memory regions No boundary to bypass

Note

The absence of any single required precondition is sufficient to invalidate the corresponding attack class.

docs.title: Role
docs.text:
• Enables execution of instructions before architectural validation
  • Required for:
    • Meltdown → transient execution of faulting loads
    • Spectre → speculative execution of mispredicted branches

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
• Instructions are issued and executed out-of-order
  • Results are temporarily stored (e.g., in ROB)
  • Commitment occurs only after validation
flowchart LR
A["Instruction Issue"] --> B["Speculative Execution"]
B --> C["Temporary State (ROB)"]
C --> D["Validation"]
D --> E["Commit or Flush"]

docs.title: Absence Effect
docs.text:
• Execution becomes strictly in-order
  • Faulting instructions stall pipeline immediately
  • No speculative side-effects are produced
Warning

Without speculative execution, both Meltdown and Spectre are fundamentally impossible.

docs.title: Role
docs.text:
• Predicts outcome of branches before resolution
  • Enables speculative execution along predicted path

Required for:

  • Spectre Variant 1 → bypass bounds checks
  • Spectre Variant 2 → redirect indirect branches

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
• Maintains prediction state (e.g., saturating counters, BTB)
  • Uses historical execution patterns

docs.title: Absence Effect
docs.text:
• Branches resolve strictly before execution continues
  • No speculative control-flow redirection possible
Note

A static or non-trainable predictor significantly reduces or eliminates Spectre attack surface.

docs.title: Role
docs.text:
• Provides a covert communication channel between:
    • Transient execution (producer)
    • Attacker measurement (consumer)

Used by:

  • Meltdown → encode leaked privileged data
  • Spectre → encode speculatively accessed data

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
• Cache state depends on memory access patterns
  • Access latency differs between:
    • Cache hit
    • Cache miss

docs.title: Absence Effect
docs.text:
• No measurable timing difference
  • No data exfiltration mechanism
Warning

Even if speculative execution exists, absence of a shared cache prevents observable leakage.

docs.title: Role
docs.text:
• Defines hardware-enforced memory isolation
  • Separates:
    • User space
    • Kernel space

Required for:

  • Meltdown → cross-boundary data access

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
• Page tables with permission bits (e.g., U/S bit)
  • Memory Management Unit (MMU)
  • Page fault exceptions on violation

docs.title: Absence Effect
docs.text:
• All memory is uniformly accessible
  • No protected region exists
  • No privilege violation possible
Note

Without a protection boundary, Meltdown has no target and becomes undefined.

docs.title: Dependency Structure
docs.text:
The attack surface can be viewed as an intersection of independent features:

graph TD
A[Speculative Execution] --> D[Transient Execution Window]
B[Branch Predictor] --> D
D --> E[Microarchitectural State Change]
C[Cache] --> E
E --> F[Observable Leakage]
G[Privilege Boundary] --> H[Meltdown Only]

docs.title: Interaction Between Preconditions
docs.text:
• Speculative execution alone → insufficient
  • Cache alone → insufficient
  • Branch prediction alone → insufficient

Attack feasibility emerges only when:

  • Execution (speculation)
  • Isolation (privilege or context boundary)
  • Observability (cache timing)

are simultaneously present.

Tip

Vulnerabilities arise from the composition of features, not individual components.

docs.title: Architectural Minimalism vs Vulnerability
docs.text:
From the analysis :

  • Removing privilege separation eliminates Meltdown
  • Removing branch prediction eliminates Spectre
  • Removing cache observability eliminates leakage

This highlights:

  • Security can be achieved by eliminating required conditions
  • Not all performance features are necessary for all system classes

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Three core requirements:

    • Speculative execution
    • Branch prediction (Spectre)
    • Shared cache

  • Additional requirement:

    • Privilege boundary (Meltdown only)

  • If any required precondition is missing:

    • The attack becomes non-instantiable

  • Vulnerability = intersection of:

    • Execution + Isolation + Observability

Architectural design choices directly determine whether transient execution attacks are possible.

docs.title: RISC-V Microcontroller Design
docs.text: Skip to TL;DR

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
This section defines the target architecture used for vulnerability evaluation. The analysis is performed on a 64-bit single-core RISC-V microcontroller designed with minimal system complexity and no operating system support.

The architectural choices directly determine the presence or absence of attack preconditions.

docs.title: Design Specification
docs.text: Parameter Specification ISA RV64I Core Type Single-core Execution Model In-order issue with OoO execution capability Pipeline Multi-stage with Reorder Buffer (ROB) Privilege Model Unprivileged (Machine mode only) Memory Model Physical addressing (no virtual memory) Cache 2-way set-associative Branch Predictor Present (e.g., 2-bit saturating / BTB) Exception Model Machine-mode exceptions only

docs.title: Execution Pipeline
docs.text:
The processor implements:

  • In-order instruction issue
  • Out-of-order execution
  • Reorder Buffer (ROB) for commit and rollback

docs.title: Execution Model
docs.text:
flowchart LR
A[Fetch] --> B[Decode]
B --> C[Issue (In-order)]
C --> D[Execute (OoO)]
D --> E[ROB]
E --> F[Commit / Flush]
  • Instructions are issued in program order
  • Execution may occur out-of-order
  • Results are committed only after validation
Note

The presence of a ROB enables speculative execution and rollback of architectural state.

docs.title: Speculative Execution Capability
docs.text:
Due to:

  • OoO execution
  • Branch prediction

The processor supports:

  • Speculative instruction execution
  • Transient data propagation

However:

  • Speculative effects are confined to microarchitectural state
  • No architectural commitment occurs until validation

docs.title: Privilege Model
docs.text:
The processor operates in a single privilege domain:

  • Machine mode only
  • No user mode (U-mode)
  • No supervisor mode (S-mode)

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• No hardware-enforced memory isolation
  • No distinction between:
    • User space
    • Kernel space
Warning

All code executes with identical privileges and unrestricted memory access.

docs.title: Addressing Model
docs.text:
• Physical addressing only
  • No virtual memory translation

docs.title: Missing Components
docs.text:
• No page tables
  • No MMU
  • No satp register
  • No page table walker

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• No address translation latency
  • No permission checks on memory access
  • No page fault exceptions
Note

Memory protection, if required, must be implemented at the software level.

docs.title: Cache Architecture
docs.text:
• 2-way set-associative cache
  • Shared across all executing code

docs.title: Properties
docs.text:
• Data-dependent cache fills
  • Measurable latency differences (hit vs miss)

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Enables timing side-channel observation
  • No hardware partitioning or isolation
Warning

The cache remains a shared observable resource and can act as a leakage channel.

docs.title: Branch Prediction
docs.text:
• Hardware branch predictor is present
  • Likely implementation:
    • 2-bit saturating counters
    • Branch Target Buffer (BTB)

docs.title: Capabilities
docs.text:
• Predicts conditional branches
  • Enables speculative execution

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Predictor state can be trained
  • Enables Spectre-style misprediction scenarios

docs.title: Exception Handling
docs.text:
• Machine-mode exceptions only
  • No user-level exception handling

docs.title: Missing Exceptions
docs.text:
• No page fault exceptions
  • No privilege violation exceptions

docs.title: Implications
docs.text:
• Faults are limited to:

  • Illegal instructions

  • Access faults (non-permission related)
Note

Absence of page faults removes the fundamental trigger required for Meltdown.

docs.title: Architectural Constraints
docs.text:
The design intentionally omits:

  • Virtual memory
  • Privilege hierarchy
  • Process isolation

This results in:

  • A flat execution environment
  • A single trust domain

docs.title: Security-Relevant Properties
docs.text: Feature Status Security Impact OoO Execution Present Enables speculation Branch Predictor Present Enables Spectre Cache Present Enables side-channel Privilege Separation Absent Eliminates Meltdown Virtual Memory Absent No protected regions Page Faults Absent No transient fault window

docs.title: Design Rationale
docs.text:
The architecture reflects a microcontroller-oriented design philosophy:

  • Minimal hardware complexity
  • Deterministic behavior
  • No OS-level abstraction

This leads to:

  • Reduced attack surface for privilege-based exploits
  • Retention of microarchitectural side-effects
Tip

The design trades isolation for simplicity, shifting security responsibility from hardware to software.

docs.title: Architectural Consequences
docs.text:
From a security perspective:

  • No hardware isolation boundary exists
  • All memory is globally accessible
  • Speculative execution remains active
  • Cache remains observable

This creates:

  • No Meltdown attack surface
  • Reduced Spectre scope
  • Persistent side-channel exposure

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• RV64I-based single-core microcontroller
  • In-order issue + OoO execution (ROB-based)
  • No virtual memory, no MMU, no page tables
  • Single privilege mode (no isolation)
  • Branch predictor and cache are present

Implications:

  • Meltdown → not applicable
  • Spectre → partially applicable
  • Cache timing side-channel → present

Info

The absence of privilege and memory protection mechanisms eliminates entire vulnerability classes but does not remove microarchitectural leakage channels.

docs.title: Vulnerability Analysis
docs.text: Skip to TL;DR

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
This section evaluates the applicability of:

  • Meltdown (CVE-2017-5754)
  • Spectre (CVE-2017-5753, CVE-2017-5715)

to the analyzed 64-bit RISC-V microcontroller design, based strictly on architectural conditions .

docs.title: Meltdown: Analysis
docs.text:
Meltdown requires:

  1. A hardware-enforced privilege boundary
  2. A faulting memory access (e.g., page fault)
  3. Speculative execution before fault resolution

docs.title: Observation
docs.text:
The target design does not implement:

  • Virtual memory
  • Page tables
  • Privilege separation (U/S modes)
  • Page fault exceptions

All memory accesses:

  • Use physical addressing
  • Are permitted without privilege checks

docs.title: Implication
docs.text:
• No access generates a permission fault
  • No protected memory region exists
  • No transient window can be created
Note

Without a fault to defer, speculative execution cannot expose unauthorized data.

docs.title: Formal Condition
docs.text:
Let:

  • (P) = protected memory domain
  • (D) = executing domain

Meltdown requires:

  • (D \neq P)
  • Access violation → fault

In this design:

  • No such domain (P) exists

Therefore:

  • Meltdown cannot be instantiated

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Property Status Privilege boundary Absent Page fault mechanism Absent Protected memory Absent Attack feasibility Not applicable Warning

Direct memory access is always permitted. This is not a Meltdown vulnerability but a consequence of the flat memory model.

docs.title: Spectre: Analysis
docs.text:
Spectre depends on:

  • Speculative execution
  • Branch misprediction
  • Cache-based side-channel

docs.title: Available Features
docs.text: Feature Status OoO execution Present Branch predictor Present Cache Present

docs.title: Requirement
docs.text:
• A security boundary between attacker and victim
  • A predictable branch (e.g., bounds check)

docs.title: Observation
docs.text:
• Single privilege domain
  • No hardware-enforced isolation
  • All memory globally accessible

docs.title: Implication
docs.text:
• No meaningful boundary exists
  • All code operates in same trust domain
Note

Leakage across a non-existent boundary is not a valid attack scenario.

docs.title: Qualification
docs.text:
If the system introduces:

  • Multiple software components
  • Shared execution without isolation

Then:

  • A software-defined boundary emerges

In such cases:

  • Spectre V1 becomes feasible
Warning

The hardware does not prevent cross-component leakage in shared execution environments.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: Scenario Status Single trust domain Not applicable Multi-component (shared memory) Conditionally possible

docs.title: Requirement
docs.text:
• Separate execution contexts (e.g., user vs kernel)
  • Shared branch predictor state
  • Indirect branch targets

docs.title: Observation
docs.text:
• No separate privilege levels
  • No isolated victim context
  • All execution occurs in same domain

docs.title: Implication
docs.text:
• No victim context to redirect
  • No meaningful BTB poisoning target
Note

Branch predictor manipulation is ineffective without context separation.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: Property Status Separate context Absent BTB attack surface Absent Attack feasibility Not applicable

docs.title: Observation
docs.text:
• 2-way set-associative cache present
  • Shared across execution
  • Measurable timing differences

docs.title: Implication
docs.text:
• Timing-based observation is possible
  • Cache can act as leakage channel

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
Property Status Cache presence Present Timing observability Present Leakage channel Potentially present Warning

Cache side-channel exists independently of Meltdown/Spectre applicability.

docs.title: Vulnerability Summary
docs.text: Vulnerability Status Reason Meltdown Not applicable No privilege boundary, no page faults Spectre V1 Conditional Requires software-defined boundary Spectre V2 Not applicable No separate execution context Cache timing channel Present Shared cache + timing

docs.title: Design-Level Interpretation
docs.text:
The vulnerability profile is determined by missing architectural features:

Missing Feature Effect Privilege separation Eliminates Meltdown Virtual memory Removes protected regions Page faults Removes transient fault window Context isolation Eliminates Spectre V2

At the same time:

Present Feature Effect OoO execution Enables speculation Branch predictor Enables Spectre V1 Cache Enables timing leakage

docs.title: Structural Insight
docs.text:
The design achieves security through absence of attack preconditions, not through mitigation.

  • No boundary → nothing to bypass
  • No fault → no transient exposure
  • No context separation → no cross-domain attack
Tip

This is an example of security by architectural minimalism, not by defensive mechanisms.

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Meltdown → Not applicable

    • No privilege boundary
    • No page fault mechanism

  • Spectre V2 → Not applicable

    • No separate execution context

  • Spectre V1 → Conditionally possible

    • Only if multiple software components share execution

  • Cache timing side-channel → Present

Vulnerability is not a function of speculative execution alone; it emerges only when combined with isolation and observability mechanisms.

docs.title: Meltdown Attack (Privileged Processors)
docs.text: Skip to TL;DR

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
This section describes the operational model of the Meltdown attack on processors that implement:

  • Virtual memory
  • Hardware privilege separation
  • Out-of-order execution

The attack exploits a transient execution window created between:

  • Memory access issuance
  • Permission check resolution

docs.title: Attack Objective
docs.text:
• Read privileged (kernel) memory from an unprivileged context
  • Bypass architectural access control using speculative execution
  • Extract data via cache timing side-channel

docs.title: Attack Preconditions
docs.text:
Meltdown requires:

Requirement Role Virtual memory (MMU) Defines protected memory Privilege levels Enforces access restrictions Page fault mechanism Delays fault handling OoO execution Enables speculative access Shared cache Enables data exfiltration Note

All conditions must be present simultaneously for the attack to succeed.

docs.title: Attack Pipeline
docs.text:
flowchart LR
A[Flush Probe Array] --> B[Illegal Kernel Load]
B --> C[Speculative Execution]
C --> D[Cache Encoding]
D --> E[Fault Raised]
E --> F[Pipeline Flush (ROB)]
F --> G[Timing Measurement]
G --> H[Secret Recovery]

docs.title: 1. Cache Preparation (Flush Phase)
docs.text:
• Attacker evicts probe array from all cache levels
  • Ensures deterministic timing behavior

Typical methods:

  • x86: clflush
  • RISC-V: cbo.inval (Zicbom) or eviction loops

docs.title: 2. Transient Faulting Load
docs.text:
The attacker executes a load from a privileged virtual address:

unsigned char secret = *(unsigned char*)kernel_addr;

docs.title: Microarchitectural Behavior
docs.text:
• MMU initiates:

    • Page table walk
    • Privilege check

  • OoO engine:

    • Executes dependent instructions before fault resolution
Warning

The load may transiently return valid data before the fault is delivered.

docs.title: 3. Speculative Data Propagation
docs.text:
The transient value is used in a dependent instruction:

temp = probe_array[secret * 512];

Effect:

  • A specific cache line is loaded
  • Secret is encoded into cache state

docs.title: 4. Fault Handling and Rollback
docs.text:
• CPU detects illegal access
  • Raises page fault exception
  • Reorder Buffer (ROB) is flushed
  • Architectural state is restored
Note

Microarchitectural state (cache contents) is not reverted.

docs.title: 5. Side-Channel Measurement (Reload Phase)
docs.text:
The attacker measures access latency:

uint64_t t0, t1;
asm volatile ("rdtsc" : "=A"(t0));
temp = probe_array[i * 512];
asm volatile ("rdtsc" : "=A"(t1));

latency = t1 - t0;

Interpretation:

  • Low latency → cache hit
  • High latency → cache miss

docs.title: 6. Secret Reconstruction
docs.text:
• Identify index with lowest latency
  • Recover corresponding byte value

Repeat:

  • For each byte in target memory
  • Sequentially reconstruct full memory region

docs.title: Timing Characteristics
docs.text:
Access Type Latency L1 Cache Hit ~4–10 cycles DRAM Access ~200–300 cycles

This difference provides:

  • High signal-to-noise ratio
  • Reliable data extraction

docs.title: Throughput
docs.text:
Empirical results (from literature):

  • ~500–2000 bytes/sec
  • ~2–8 seconds for 4 KB memory page
Note

Throughput depends on noise, cache hierarchy, and system load.

docs.title: Key Mechanism
docs.text:
The attack relies on:

Transient availability of unauthorized data before permission enforcement

Combined with:

Non-rollback of microarchitectural state

docs.title: Why It Works
docs.text:
The CPU prioritizes performance:

  • Executes instructions speculatively
  • Defers exception handling

This creates:

  • A temporal gap between:

    • Data access
    • Access validation

During this gap:

  • Sensitive data becomes transiently usable

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
Meltdown is effective only when:

  • Memory protection exists
  • Faults are deferred
  • Speculative execution is deep enough

It does not apply when:

  • No privilege boundary exists
  • No page faults occur
  • Execution is strictly in-order

docs.title: Relation to RISC-V MCU
docs.text:
In contrast to the analyzed microcontroller:

  • No MMU → no page table walk
  • No privilege separation → no protected memory
  • No page faults → no transient fault window
Warning

The Meltdown sequence cannot be instantiated without a faulting access condition.

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Exploits speculative execution before permission checks resolve

  • Reads kernel memory from user space

  • Uses cache timing side-channel (Flush+Reload)

  • Requires:

    • MMU + page tables
    • Privilege separation
    • Page faults
    • OoO execution

  • Fails if:

    • No protection boundary exists
    • No fault is generated

Meltdown is fundamentally a race between data access and permission enforcement, observable via cache state.

docs.title: RISC-V Failure Analysis
docs.text: Skip to TL;DR

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
This section constructs hypothetical Meltdown and Spectre attack attempts on the analyzed RISC-V microcontroller and traces their execution through the pipeline.

The objective is to identify the exact failure point of each attack based on architectural constraints .

docs.title: Attack Setup
docs.text:
Assume:

  • Attacker attempts to read memory at address 0x80001000
  • Memory is logically considered sensitive (software convention)
  • Standard Meltdown sequence is executed

docs.title: Execution Trace
docs.text: Step Action Outcome 1 Flush probe array Achievable (via eviction loop if needed) 2 Load from target address Succeeds architecturally 3 Speculative encode Redundant 4 Timing measurement Possible 5 Secret recovery Directly available

docs.title: Critical Observation
docs.text:
flowchart LR
A[Load Instruction] --> B[Physical Address Access]
B --> C[Data Returned]
C --> D[No Fault Generated]
D --> E[No Speculative Window]
  • No page table walk
  • No privilege check
  • No exception generated
Warning

The load does not fault. Therefore, no transient execution window is created.

docs.title: Root Cause of Failure
docs.text:
Meltdown requires:

  • Faulting access + delayed exception

In this design:

  • Memory access is always valid
  • No protection boundary exists

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
• Data is returned architecturally, not transiently
  • No need for speculative bypass

docs.title: Key Finding
docs.text:
The attack succeeds as a normal memory read, not as a Meltdown attack.

Note

This is not a vulnerability—it is a consequence of the flat memory model.

docs.title: Formal Interpretation
docs.text:
Let:

  • (M) = Meltdown attack
  • (P) = protected memory domain

Requirement:

  • (\exists P) such that access causes fault

In this system:

  • (P) does not exist

Therefore:

  • (M) is undefined

docs.title: Attack Setup
docs.text:
Assume:

  • Multiple software tasks (A and B) share execution
  • Shared function:
void process_input(uint32_t x) {
    if (x < buffer_size) {
        val = buffer[x];
        temp = probe[val * 512];
    }
}

docs.title: Execution Trace
docs.text: Step Action Outcome 1 Train branch predictor Succeeds 2 Mispredict branch Succeeds 3 Speculative access Succeeds 4 Cache encode Succeeds 5 Timing measurement Succeeds 6 Secret recovery Succeeds

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
flowchart LR
A[Train Predictor] --> B[Mispredict]
B --> C[Speculative Access]
C --> D[Cache Update]
D --> E[Rollback]
E --> F[Timing Leak]

docs.title: Key Observation
docs.text:
• No hardware memory protection
  • Tasks share same physical address space
Warning

Speculative access to another task’s data is not blocked by hardware.

docs.title: Result
docs.text: Condition Outcome Single trust domain No meaningful attack Multiple components Attack possible

docs.title: Interpretation
docs.text:
• Attack is not prevented by hardware
  • Feasibility depends on software-level isolation
Note

Spectre V1 is valid only if a logical boundary exists between software components.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
• Separate execution contexts
  • Shared predictor state
  • Indirect branch targets

docs.title: Observation
docs.text:
• Single privilege level
  • No context isolation
  • No victim domain

docs.title: Execution Breakdown
docs.text: Step Action Outcome 1 Poison BTB Possible 2 Redirect victim execution No valid target 3 Execute gadget Not applicable 4 Leak data Not applicable

docs.title: Root Cause of Failure
docs.text:
flowchart LR
A[Single Execution Context] --> B[No Victim Isolation]
B --> C[No Redirection Target]
C --> D[Attack Fails]
  • No distinct victim context exists
  • All execution occurs in same domain

docs.title: Result
docs.text: • Spectre V2 is not applicable

docs.title: Observation
docs.text:
• Cache is shared
  • Timing differences measurable

docs.title: Implication
docs.text:
• Side-channel remains functional
Note

Cache timing leakage is independent of Meltdown/Spectre applicability.

docs.title: Direct Comparison
docs.text: Property RISC-V MCU Privileged CPU Privilege boundary Absent Present Page faults Absent Present Direct memory read Allowed Restricted Speculative execution Present Present Cache side-channel Present Present Meltdown Not applicable Exploitable Spectre V1 Conditional Exploitable Spectre V2 Not applicable Exploitable

docs.title: Structural Reasoning
docs.text:
The failure of attacks is due to:

Missing Feature Effect Privilege boundary No Meltdown target Page fault mechanism No transient fault window Context isolation No Spectre V2 target

docs.title: Key Insight
docs.text:
The system does not mitigate attacks—it eliminates their preconditions.

Tip

This represents security through architectural simplification rather than defensive mechanisms.

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Meltdown:

    • Fails because no fault occurs
    • Memory access succeeds directly

  • Spectre V1:

    • Possible only with shared software components
    • Not prevented by hardware

  • Spectre V2:

    • Fails due to lack of context separation

  • Cache side-channel:

    • Present and observable

The microcontroller is immune to Meltdown and partially exposed to Spectre solely due to its architectural design choices.

docs.title: Spectre Attack (Privileged Processors)
docs.text: Skip to TL;DR

docs.title: Context
docs.text:
This section describes the operational model of Spectre attacks on processors implementing:

  • Speculative execution
  • Branch prediction
  • Shared microarchitectural state

Unlike Meltdown, Spectre manipulates control flow speculation rather than exploiting a faulting access.

docs.title: Attack Objective
docs.text:
• Force a victim to execute attacker-influenced speculative paths
  • Access sensitive data during speculation
  • Leak data via cache timing side-channel

docs.title: Attack Preconditions
docs.text:
Spectre requires:

Requirement Role Branch predictor Enables misprediction Speculative execution Executes unintended path Shared cache Encodes leaked data Predictable victim code Provides exploitable gadget

For Variant 2:

Additional Requirement Role Shared predictor state Enables cross-context poisoning Indirect branches Required for BTB attack

docs.title: Victim Code Pattern
docs.text:
if (x < array1_size) {
    uint8_t val = array1[x];
    temp = probe_array[val * 512];
}

docs.title: Execution Flow (Variant 1)
docs.text:
flowchart LR
A[Train Predictor] --> B[Send Malicious Input]
B --> C[Branch Misprediction]
C --> D[Speculative Execution]
D --> E[Out-of-Bounds Access]
E --> F[Cache Encoding]
F --> G[Rollback]
G --> H[Timing Measurement]

docs.title: 1. Branch Predictor Training
docs.text:
• Attacker repeatedly invokes victim with valid inputs

  • Branch predictor learns:

    • Condition is true (taken)

docs.title: 2. Misprediction Trigger
docs.text:
• Attacker supplies out-of-bounds index x
  • Predictor still assumes branch is taken

Result:

  • Speculative execution enters guarded block

docs.title: 3. Speculative Out-of-Bounds Access
docs.text:
val = array1[x];   // x is attacker-controlled
  • Access may reference sensitive memory
  • Occurs only transiently

docs.title: 4. Cache Encoding
docs.text:
temp = probe_array[val * 512];
  • Secret-dependent memory access
  • Corresponding cache line is loaded

docs.title: 5. Rollback
docs.text:
• Branch resolves as false
  • Speculative instructions are discarded
  • Architectural state is restored
Note

Cache state persists despite rollback.

docs.title: 6. Data Exfiltration
docs.text:
• Attacker measures probe array access latency
  • Fast access → identifies val

docs.title: Mechanism
docs.text:
• Targets indirect branches
  • Attacker poisons Branch Target Buffer (BTB)

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text:
flowchart LR
A[Poison BTB] --> B[Victim Indirect Branch]
B --> C[Speculative Jump to Gadget]
C --> D[Secret Access]
D --> E[Cache Encoding]
E --> F[Rollback]
F --> G[Timing Measurement]

docs.title: Step-by-Step
docs.text:
1. Attacker trains BTB with malicious target
  2. Victim executes indirect branch
  3. Predictor redirects execution to attacker-controlled gadget
  4. Gadget accesses sensitive data
  5. Cache encodes data
  6. Attacker extracts via timing
Warning

Variant 2 enables cross-context attacks (e.g., user → kernel).

docs.title: Timing Side-Channel Integration
docs.text:
Both variants rely on:

  • Cache state modification
  • Timing-based observation

Measurement example (RISC-V):

uint64_t t0, t1;
asm volatile ("rdcycle %0" : "=r"(t0));
volatile uint8_t val = probe_array[i * 512];
asm volatile ("rdcycle %0" : "=r"(t1));

latency = t1 - t0;

docs.title: Key Mechanism
docs.text:
Spectre exploits:

Mispredicted control flow leading to transient execution of unintended instructions

Combined with:

Persistent microarchitectural side-effects

docs.title: Why It Works
docs.text:
• Branch prediction assumes past behavior predicts future
  • Speculative execution executes ahead of validation
  • No isolation of predictor state across contexts

This allows:

  • Attacker-controlled influence over execution path
  • Leakage of data without violating access permissions

docs.title: Differences from Meltdown
docs.text: Property Spectre Meltdown Trigger Branch misprediction Faulting load Requires privilege violation No Yes Target Control flow Memory protection Scope Same/cross context Cross-privilege

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
Spectre requires:

  • Predictable victim code
  • Shared microarchitectural resources
  • Sufficient speculation depth

It does not:

  • Directly bypass hardware permissions
  • Work without a side-channel

docs.title: Relation to RISC-V MCU
docs.text:
In the analyzed microcontroller:

  • Branch predictor → Present
  • OoO execution → Present
  • Cache → Present

However:

  • No privilege separation
  • No process isolation

Implications:

  • Spectre V2 → Not applicable
  • Spectre V1 → Only relevant in multi-component software systems
Note

The attack depends on existence of a meaningful isolation boundary.

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Exploits branch misprediction + speculative execution

  • Forces execution of unintended code paths

  • Leaks data via cache timing

  • Variant 1:

    • Bounds check bypass
    • Same-context attack

  • Variant 2:

    • Branch target injection
    • Cross-context attack

  • Requires:

    • Branch predictor
    • Speculation
    • Shared cache

Spectre is a control-flow manipulation attack that leverages speculation to expose data without violating architectural access rules.

docs.title: Meltdown
docs.text: Skip to TL;DR

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Meltdown (CVE-2017-5754) is a transient execution attack that exploits the delay between:

  • Speculative execution of instructions
  • Enforcement of memory access permissions

It allows an unprivileged context to read privileged memory by leveraging microarchitectural side-effects, primarily cache state changes.

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text:
Modern processors perform out-of-order (OoO) execution to improve performance. Instructions are executed before it is fully verified whether they are:

  • Architecturally valid
  • Permitted by privilege rules

For a memory load:

  1. The CPU issues the load
  2. Permission checks (via MMU/page tables) are still in progress
  3. Dependent instructions may execute speculatively

If the access is illegal:

  • A fault is eventually raised
  • Architectural state is rolled back
  • Microarchitectural state is not reverted

This creates a transient window where unauthorized data is usable.

Note

The vulnerability arises from the lack of synchronization between data access and permission validation.

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text:
Meltdown operates in four stages:

flowchart LR
A[Flush Cache] --> B[Illegal Memory Load]
B --> C[Speculative Use of Data]
C --> D[Cache Encoding]
D --> E[Fault Raised]
E --> F[Architectural Rollback]
F --> G[Timing Measurement]

docs.title: 1. Cache Preparation
docs.text:
The attacker evicts a probe array from the cache:

  • Ensures all accesses result in cache misses initially
  • Provides a clean baseline for timing measurement

docs.title: 2. Faulting Load
docs.text:
A load instruction targets a privileged address:

  • Example: kernel virtual address
  • Access violates privilege rules

However:

  • The load may still return data transiently
  • Execution continues speculatively

docs.title: 3. Transient Execution
docs.text:
The loaded value is used in dependent instructions:

  • Typically as an index into a probe array
  • Example:
temp = probe_array[secret * 512];

This causes:

  • A specific cache line to be loaded
  • Encoding of the secret into cache state

docs.title: 4. Exception and Rollback
docs.text:
• The CPU detects the illegal access
  • Raises a page fault exception
  • Flushes pipeline and restores architectural state
Note

The cache modification caused during speculative execution is not reverted.

docs.title: 5. Data Exfiltration
docs.text:
The attacker measures access time to probe array entries:

  • Cache hit → low latency (~L1 access)
  • Cache miss → high latency (DRAM access)

The fastest access reveals the secret value.

docs.title: Required Architectural Conditions
docs.text:
Meltdown requires all of the following:

Requirement Role Out-of-order execution Enables speculative execution before fault resolution Virtual memory (MMU) Defines protected vs unprotected regions Privilege levels Enforces access restrictions Page fault mechanism Delays fault handling Shared cache Enables side-channel leakage Warning

Absence of any of these conditions prevents Meltdown from being instantiated.

docs.title: Affected Systems
docs.text:
Meltdown primarily affects:

  • Intel processors (post-1995, OoO-based)
  • Selected ARM cores (with similar execution behavior)

Processors with:

  • Strict in-order execution
  • Early permission checks
  • No shared cache

are generally not vulnerable.

docs.title: Key Property
docs.text:
The defining characteristic of Meltdown is:

Unauthorized data is transiently accessible before the CPU enforces access control.

This is not a software bug but a consequence of:

  • Microarchitectural optimization
  • Deferred exception handling

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
Meltdown specifically targets:

  • Cross-privilege data leakage (user → kernel)

It does not inherently:

  • Require branch prediction
  • Depend on attacker-controlled victim code
  • Operate across identical privilege domains

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Exploits delay between speculative execution and permission checks
  • Reads privileged memory from unprivileged context
  • Uses cache timing side-channel for data exfiltration

  • Requires:

    • OoO execution
    • Virtual memory + page tables
    • Privilege separation

  • Not applicable in systems without hardware-enforced memory protection

Meltdown is fundamentally a privilege boundary violation enabled by transient execution, not merely a cache side-channel attack.

docs.title: Cache Timing Side-Channel
docs.text: Skip to TL;DR

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
A cache timing side-channel is a microarchitectural leakage mechanism that exposes information through timing differences in memory access latency.

It is the primary data exfiltration channel used by both Meltdown and Spectre .

docs.title: Core Principle
docs.text:
Memory access latency depends on where the data resides:

Location Typical Latency L1 Cache ~4–15 cycles DRAM ~150–300 cycles

This timing difference is:

  • Measurable via high-resolution timers
  • Deterministic enough to encode information
Note

The attacker does not read data directly; instead, it infers data based on access time.

docs.title: Information Encoding Mechanism
docs.text:
Sensitive data is encoded into cache state using a probe array:

temp = probe_array[secret * STRIDE];
  • secret determines which cache line is accessed
  • That cache line becomes resident (cached)
  • Other lines remain uncached

This creates a one-to-one mapping between secret value and cache state

docs.title: Flush+Reload Technique
docs.text: The most commonly used technique is Flush+Reload .

docs.title: Execution Flow
docs.text:
flowchart LR
A[Flush Cache Line] --> B[Victim Access]
B --> C[Cache Line Loaded]
C --> D[Measure Access Time]
D --> E[Infer Cached Index]

docs.title: 1. Flush Phase
docs.text:
• Attacker evicts probe array from cache
  • On x86: clflush
  • On RISC-V: cbo.inval (Zicbom) or eviction loop

docs.title: 2. Victim / Speculative Access
docs.text:
• Victim or transient execution accesses:
probe_array[secret * STRIDE];
  • Corresponding cache line is loaded

docs.title: 3. Reload Phase
docs.text:
• Attacker measures access time for each index:
uint64_t t0, t1;
asm volatile ("rdcycle %0" : "=r"(t0));
volatile uint8_t val = probe_array[i * STRIDE];
asm volatile ("rdcycle %0" : "=r"(t1));

latency = t1 - t0;
  • Fast access → cache hit
  • Slow access → cache miss

docs.title: 4. Secret Recovery
docs.text:
• Index with lowest latency corresponds to secret
Tip

Only one cache line is significantly faster, enabling reliable identification of the encoded value.

docs.title: Why It Works
docs.text:
The attack relies on a fundamental asymmetry:

  • Architectural state is rolled back after faults or misprediction
  • Microarchitectural state (cache) is not
Warning

Cache contents persist even if the instruction that caused the access is never committed.

This property enables:

  • Meltdown → leak across privilege boundary
  • Spectre → leak across control-flow boundary

docs.title: Alternative Techniques
docs.text: While Flush+Reload requires shared memory, other techniques exist:

docs.title: Prime+Probe
docs.text:
• Attacker fills cache sets (prime)
  • Victim evicts lines
  • Attacker measures which sets were evicted

docs.title: Flush+Flush
docs.text:
• Measures time taken to flush cache lines
  • Faster and stealthier than Flush+Reload
Note

These techniques do not require shared memory, making them applicable in broader scenarios.

docs.title: RISC-V Timing Measurement
docs.text:
On RISC-V, timing is typically measured using the cycle counter:

uint64_t t0, t1;
asm volatile ("rdcycle %0" : "=r"(t0));
volatile uint8_t val = probe_array[i * 512];
asm volatile ("rdcycle %0" : "=r"(t1));

uint64_t latency = t1 - t0;

Typical threshold:

  • Cache hit: < ~15 cycles
  • Cache miss: > ~150 cycles
Warning

The rdcycle CSR is accessible in unprivileged mode unless explicitly restricted, enabling high-resolution timing attacks.

docs.title: Required Conditions
docs.text:
A cache timing side-channel requires:

Condition Role Shared cache Enables observation of victim activity Timing source Measures access latency Deterministic cache behavior Ensures repeatability Data-dependent memory access Encodes secret into cache

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
The channel depends on:

  • Noise (interrupts, scheduling)
  • Cache associativity and replacement policy
  • Timer resolution

It does not:

  • Provide direct memory access
  • Work without a measurable timing difference

docs.title: Role in Meltdown and Spectre
docs.text:
Attack Role of Side-Channel Meltdown Extracts transiently accessed privileged data Spectre Extracts speculatively accessed data Info

The side-channel is not the vulnerability itself; it is the mechanism that converts transient execution effects into observable data.

docs.title: Architectural Insight
docs.text:
The vulnerability arises from:

  • Performance optimizations (caching, speculation)
  • Lack of rollback for microarchitectural state

This creates a persistent channel:

  • Invisible at ISA level
  • Observable via timing

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Cache timing side-channel leaks data via access latency differences
  • Uses techniques like Flush+Reload and Prime+Probe
  • Encodes secrets into cache state via data-dependent access
  • Requires:
    • Shared cache
    • High-resolution timer
  • Not rolled back after speculative execution
  • Enables data exfiltration in both Meltdown and Spectre

The cache acts as a covert communication channel between transient execution and attacker observation.

docs.title: Spectre
docs.text: Skip to TL;DR

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
Spectre is a class of transient execution attacks that exploit speculative execution triggered by branch misprediction.

Unlike Meltdown, Spectre does not require a privilege violation. Instead, it causes a victim to speculatively execute unintended instructions, which leak sensitive data through microarchitectural side-effects.

docs.title: Root Cause
docs.text:
Modern processors use branch prediction to improve performance:

  • Predict the outcome of conditional branches
  • Speculatively execute instructions before the branch resolves

If the prediction is incorrect:

  • Speculative results are discarded architecturally
  • Microarchitectural side-effects remain

This allows an attacker to:

  • Influence the prediction mechanism
  • Control speculative execution paths
  • Extract data via cache timing
Note

Spectre exploits correctly implemented performance optimizations rather than violating access permissions.

docs.title: Variant 1 — Bounds Check Bypass (CVE-2017-5753)
docs.text:
• Exploits conditional branches (e.g., bounds checks)
  • Trains predictor to assume branch is always taken
  • Causes speculative execution of out-of-bounds access

docs.title: Variant 2 — Branch Target Injection (CVE-2017-5715)
docs.text:
• Exploits indirect branch prediction (BTB poisoning)
  • Redirects speculative execution to attacker-controlled gadgets
  • Enables cross-context data leakage
Note

Variant 1 operates within the same context. Variant 2 targets cross-context or cross-privilege execution.

docs.title: Execution Flow (Variant 1)
docs.text:
flowchart LR
A[Train Branch Predictor] --> B[Send Malicious Input]
B --> C[Branch Misprediction]
C --> D[Speculative Execution]
D --> E[Out-of-Bounds Access]
E --> F[Cache Encoding]
F --> G[Rollback]
G --> H[Timing Measurement]

docs.title: 1. Predictor Training
docs.text:
The attacker repeatedly executes a branch with valid inputs:

if (x < size) { ... }
  • Branch predictor learns: branch is taken

docs.title: 2. Misprediction Trigger
docs.text:
The attacker supplies an out-of-bounds index:

  • Predictor still assumes branch is taken
  • Speculative execution enters the branch body

docs.title: 3. Transient Execution
docs.text:
Speculative path performs:

value = array[x];                  // out-of-bounds read
temp = probe[value * 512];         // encode into cache
  • Secret value is used to access probe array
  • Cache state is modified

docs.title: 4. Rollback
docs.text:
• Branch resolves as incorrect
  • Speculative instructions are discarded
  • Architectural state is restored
Note

Cache modifications persist despite rollback.

docs.title: 5. Data Exfiltration
docs.text:
The attacker measures access time to probe array:

  • Fast access → cached → reveals secret
  • Slow access → not cached

docs.title: Variant 2 (High-Level)
docs.text:
Variant 2 differs in mechanism:

  • Targets indirect branches
  • Attacker poisons Branch Target Buffer (BTB)
  • Victim speculatively jumps to attacker-chosen code

This enables:

  • Execution of gadgets
  • Leakage of sensitive data across contexts
Warning

Variant 2 requires shared predictor state across execution contexts.

docs.title: Required Architectural Conditions
docs.text:
Spectre requires:

Requirement Role Branch predictor Enables misprediction Speculative execution Executes unintended instructions Shared cache Provides timing side-channel Predictable victim code Provides exploitable gadget

For Variant 2:

Additional Requirement Role Shared predictor state Enables cross-context poisoning Indirect branches Required for BTB manipulation

docs.title: Key Property
docs.text:
The defining characteristic of Spectre is:

Speculative execution follows attacker-influenced paths within otherwise valid code.

No access violation is required.

docs.title: Differences from Meltdown
docs.text: Property Spectre Meltdown Requires privilege violation No Yes Trigger mechanism Branch misprediction Faulting load Target Victim code paths Protected memory Scope Same or cross-context Cross-privilege

docs.title: Limitations
docs.text:
Spectre requires:

  • A predictable victim code pattern
  • A shared microarchitectural resource (cache, predictor)

It does not:

  • Directly bypass hardware memory protection
  • Guarantee leakage without a usable side-channel

docs.title: TL;DR
docs.text:
• Exploits branch misprediction + speculative execution
  • Causes execution of unintended code paths
  • Leaks data via cache timing side-channel

  • Variant 1:

    • Bounds check bypass
    • Same-context attack

  • Variant 2:

    • Branch target injection
    • Cross-context attack

  • Requires:

    • Branch predictor
    • Speculative execution
    • Shared cache

Spectre is a control-flow manipulation attack that leverages speculative execution to expose data, rather than violating memory access permissions.
